interest: The interest behind this argument is that of equity-oriented reform advocates, disadvantaged/Roma pupils, families of young children, teachers, and reform-realist governance actors who want early sorting genuinely removed rather than relocated.
value: The value in tension is structural equity (removing the sorting mechanism at its root) versus middle-class choice, institutional stability, and deliverable teacher capacity.
fear: The driving fear is that abolishing comprehensive-school selection triggers middle-class and church/foundation-school flight that reconcentrates disadvantage in remaining public schools, reproducing the SES gradient in a new institutional form while teachers absorb unfunded transition risk.
affected: public comprehensive school teachers and unions
assumption: The claim rests on the unstated assumption that choice-architecture and maintainer-transparency safeguards can actually be legislated, enforced, and sustained across electoral cycles well enough to prevent sorting migration.
empirical_uncertainty: The cross-national delayed-tracking evidence (Hanushek & Woessmann 2006) is strong, but the Hungarian peer-effect mechanism is contested/weak, teacher-retraining cost/capacity has no precise per-cohort figure (confidence low), and the magnitude of sorting migration and political durability across two cycles has no clear Hungarian precedent.
